does: This paper investigates mechanistic unfaithfulness in transcoders—systems designed to decompose neural network activations into sparse, interpretable components—through a controlled toy model. The authors construct a minimal setting where a transcoder faithfully represents some mechanisms while failing to capture others, allowing them to characterize when and why transcoders produce misleading interpretations. They analyze how the transcoder's optimization objectives and architectural constraints lead to systematic distortions in which features get represented, demonstrating that transcoders can create false narratives about model behavior even when their reconstructions appear accurate by conventional metrics.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature in mechanistic interpretability that seeks to understand neural networks through sparse feature decomposition, extending recent research on transcoders as tools for finding interpretable bases in activation spaces. It connects to longstanding concerns about faithfulness in interpretability methods—the question of whether extracted explanations genuinely reflect the model's actual computations or merely post-hoc rationalizations. The paper responds to empirical observations that transcoders sometimes recover features that appear to have little causal role in the model's behavior, and it engages with broader debates about the limitations of unsupervised representation learning for interpretability purposes.
enables: This work establishes foundations for developing more reliable interpretability methods by identifying specific failure modes and conditions under which faithfulness breaks down. It suggests future directions in designing better objectives or constraints for transcoders that can guarantee or enforce higher fidelity to actual model mechanisms. The analysis opens pathways for developing diagnostic tools to detect when extracted features are faithfully representing computations versus when they are artifacts of optimization, potentially enabling mechanistic interpretability methods that can be trusted for causal analysis of neural network behavior and more robust explanations of model failures.
